data.title: Lyft: High Growth And Low Leverage Make This Undervalued Turnaround Hard To Ignore
data.description:
Lyft, Inc. (LYFT) is a ridesharing platform, operating as a peer-to-peer marketplace, connecting riders and drivers via its application in primarily the US and Canada, yet new locations are expanding the company's footprint. LYFT also offers car and scooter rental services, bike sharing, and others.

Although the stock appreciated by roughly 28% over the past 12 months and significantly outperformed the benchmark, it remains cheap. Lyft is trading at a forward P/E of approximately 19, suggesting a 6% discount to peers at 20x, and roughly 20% undervaluation compared to the average company in the S&P500 at a 23x earnings multiple. The ridesharing platform remains a high-growth story with significant tailwinds, arguing for a bullish continuation.

I think that Lyft may appear compelling for long-term investors who may seek diversification into a high-growth name with a solid moat and future tailwinds at an appealing price tag. I rate Lyft a Buy with a $28 price target over the next 12 months, indicating a 16% upside possibility.

FQ3 2025: Despite Double Miss, Top- And Bottom-Line Growth Remains Intact

On November 5, the company delivered FQ3 2025 results. Although on the surface it could appear as a weak quarterly performance for the ridesharing platform due to a double miss, it was actually not. Lyft posted top- and bottom-line growth, highlighting that the positive trend is still intact, and record gross bookings, implying a solid position in the market. The market reacted to the results with a 3.40% upside on the earnings day.

Lyft posted $1.69 billion in revenue, up 11% on a year-over-year basis, but $13 million below what the market had anticipated. The bottom-line arrived at $0.30 in diluted EPS, an approximately 3% increase versus $0.29 in the same quarter last year, yet $0.01 below analysts' estimates.

In the earnings presentation, management highlighted a surge in gross bookings, reaching $4.8 billion in the quarter, implying a significant 16% growth on a year-over-year basis. The company achieved 2.9% in adjusted EBITDA margin as a percentage of gross bookings. On a trailing twelve-month basis, Lyft generated $1.03 billion in free cash flow. These are outstanding results, highlighting a solid position in the ridesharing platform and demand for the company's services.

The ridesharing platform had 28.7 million active riders in the quarter, representing an all-time high result, and 18% growth over the past 12 months. This led to the 248.8 million in total rides for FQ3 2025, a 15% rise on a year-over-year basis, and another all-time high performance. I think these results highlight excellent execution by management, and if the trend of such rapid growth with solid demand continues, I believe the market may reprice the stock at a higher premium, supporting my bullish narrative.

Partnerships, I believe, are one of the key tailwinds supporting the company's future success. Over the past months, the ridesharing application has announced meaningful partnerships. For instance, on September 17, Lyft and Waymo, which is the leading autonomous driving technology company, launched a partnership to expand autonomous mobility in Nashville. Based on this, the companies will leverage Lyft's integrated fleet management services and are expected to offer rides first through the Waymo app and then via Lyft later in FY2026.

Furthermore, Lyft also partnered with Curb to enhance driver opportunities and optimize the marketplace. Curb is the leading platform for licensed taxis, and this partnership will allow riders a more seamless experience, significantly contributing to the thesis of a more sticky customer base and long-term growth. In the earnings call, Lyft also highlighted that in FQ3 2025, the ridesharing company experienced the highest partnerships-linked drives ever.

I think these partnerships may heavily contribute to the company's future success. Highlighting a partnership with Waymo, riders may be excited to use autonomous mobility services in the area, and that could result in a revenue surge for Lyft. Partnership with Curb may result in a better overall experience for users, laying the foundation for future years' success. Based on the statement, this quarter reflected the highest amount of partner-linked rides ever, which suggests the company's strategy translates into meaningful top and bottom-line growth, supporting a bullish narrative.

Management has guided the upcoming FQ4 2025. The ridesharing company anticipates $5.06 billion in gross bookings at the midpoint, reflecting an approximately 18% growth on a year-over-year basis. The adjusted EBITDA is expected to arrive at $145 million at the midpoint, highlighting a roughly 2.85% percentage of gross bookings. The company remains on track for both top and bottom-line expansion, including margins. I think LYFT appears well-positioned to achieve the targets, highlighting a solid momentum in demand and continued partnerships, which could accelerate business performance.

To conclude, this has been an outstanding quarter for the company, representing a solid top and bottom-line growth, with significant tailwinds related to key partnerships, new locations, such as European countries, and a strategy aiming for a satisfactory user experience, which may lead to a sticky customer base. The robust business performance and excellent execution remain intact, and this supports my thesis of the continuation of the upward momentum.

A High-Growth, High-Demand Company, Yet Trades At A Discount To Market

Lyft currently trades at a forward P/E of about 19, which, as mentioned above, indicates a discount to both peers and the average company in the S&P500. Given the solid quarterly results, this creates a disconnect in valuation. I think that if the growth delivers, this gap may narrow over the following quarters, arguing for more upside and closer to the benchmark valuation.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 0.46, Lyft appears roughly 3.7x undervalued versus the sector median of 1.82, highlighting the gap and supporting my bullish outlook. Moreover, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 1.25 and 1.36, respectively, underline a 50% undervaluation at the midpoint compared to peers at 2.16 and 1.68. This suggests that the market has not priced in future revenue growth, and if the company continues to deliver, the stock price may mirror significant performance.

The company has a low-leveraged capital structure, and that is a substantial argument for a positive continuation. The ridesharing platform has $1.32 billion in total obligations and $1.99 billion in cash; therefore, Lyft could pay its debt with ease. This is appealing, as it suggests financial flexibility for management.

Lyft remains a phenomenal revenue growth story, so far. Over the past 12 months, the ridesharing platform achieved roughly 15% revenue growth, arguing for 3.5x outperformance versus peers at 4.05%. The forward revenue growth rate of 18.64% argues that this positive trend is likely to continue its upward momentum, representing an approximately 3.8x outperformance compared to the sector median of nearly 5%.

Based on the previous quarterly results, the bottom line grew at roughly a mid-single-digit pace, which may appear insignificant, but the future outlook, underscoring a solid demand, continued riders growth, and key partnerships, may significantly contribute to its turnaround phase and accelerate the expansion of diluted EPS.

Although profitability metrics indicate a mixed bag, overall, it presents market-like operating margins, which would justify a closer-to-industry valuation. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 35% reflects a 14% advantage over peers at 31%. The net income margin (TTM) of 2.40% balances the bullish narrative, indicating a 2x underperformance versus peers at 6.29%.

Management delivered 24% return on common equity (TTM), a 2x outperformance versus peers at 12%, and generated $1.08 billion in cash from operations (TTM), suggesting a roughly 3x outperformance versus peers at $362 million.

To conclude, Lyft remains a high-quality company, which is experiencing steady top- and bottom-line growth. The ridesharing platform operates at industry-like margins, arguing for closer-to-peers valuation, supporting a bullish narrative. Lyft remains a low-leveraged business, and that presents a positive tailwind. Based on the analysis, the market has not priced in the future revenue growth, and if momentum in solid business performance continues, I think investors may reprice the stock at a higher premium.

Wall Street anticipates $1.15 in diluted EPS for FY2025, and $1.48 for FY2026. I agree with the following estimates, based on the tailwinds highlighted in the earnings overview and valuation analysis. Lyft remains well-positioned to ride its future tailwinds, and with solid momentum in demand, I think the ridesharing company may achieve its targets.

If we apply a 19x earnings multiple to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $1.48, we arrive at my price target of $28. This would represent the current earnings multiple, and the stock price appreciation mirroring bottom-line growth. Although I believe Lyft deserves a higher valuation, I remain cautious and favor a more conservative approach, leaving me room for error.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Lyft that could meaningfully impact the stock price performance in the near term.

Lyft remains the second-largest ridesharing platform in the US after Uber. Based on the article, the company had roughly 26% of the market in 2023, and I would anticipate Lyft has sustained a relatively similar portion of the market to this date. The market may view Uber as the winner, which may limit upside in the near term, resulting in investors favoring a market-leading competitor. This remains a headwind for years, as Uber's dominance is likely to continue in the years to come.

Insurance remains a significant expense, and any changes in regulation or adverse claims could heavily compress the company's margins. I think this remains an area to monitor for long-term investors. In addition, there may be additional regulatory concerns tied to minimum wage mandates, city-specific fees, etc., which could change over time, erode the company's performance, and cloud the future outlook.

The company is heavily concentrated on a single revenue stream, which may weigh on the stock. If the economy enters a recession or a shock occurs tied to rider/driver supply, Lyft's performance would likely be severely affected.

On the other hand, if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, Lyft appears well-positioned to ride the tailwinds.

Conclusion: Lyft Is A Buy

I think Lyft may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who seek exposure to autonomous mobility, which is anticipated to experience significant double-digit growth over the following years. Based on the valuation analysis, the company appears to be trading at an appealing valuation, which may suggest the downside could be limited, and Lyft is well-positioned to continue a solid upward momentum in the stock price.

Although the company experiences substantial competition from Uber, it continues to steadily grow its top and bottom line, which argues for a bullish narrative. Lyft remains a low-leveraged business, which leaves financial flexibility for management, and signs new partnerships with different businesses, supporting its future success.

I think Lyft is a Buy with a $28 price target over the next 12 months, suggesting a 16% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. 46 Wall Street analysts argue for a $23 price target over the next year, highlighting a nearly 4% downside risk. I feel more bullish, but such an outcome is definitely possible if major headwinds occur.
